scope.area: HTTP request execution pipeline and response flow

summary.highLevelVerdict: Compared with the first HTTP snapshot, several important structural problems are fixed: route resolution now happens early, RequestData and ResponseData allow the needed mutations, static streaming and controller-response mapping are much healthier, and the uWS limiter compatibility issue is resolved. However the live default pipeline is still blocked by one active stage bug and two incomplete stateful paths: mid-queue-stage never returns true and therefore stops the pipeline immediately, session payloads are fetched but never attached to controller context, and route/response caching is only partially implemented.

transportEntryAndRouteResolution.question: Does the active uWS adapter now enter the HTTP flow and resolve tenant routes at the right point?
transportEntryAndRouteResolution.answer: Yes. Requests enter through the active uWS catch-all handler, execution context creation happens immediately, request metadata is normalized first, and manager route resolution now runs before body parsing and before pipeline execution. Missing routes also now produce a normal 404 response instead of falling through later logic.

evidence.note: uWS catch-all entry creates an execution context and dispatches request hooks.
Request setup happens before manager.resolveRoute(), and unresolved routes now return 404 Not Found early.
The manager facade now assigns the resolved TenantRoute back into executionContext.tenantRoute.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Each HTTP request creates an execution context
Tenant route resolution happens before body parsing and pipeline execution
Unresolved tenant routes produce a normal 404 response

requestAndResponseContracts.question: Are the request and response envelopes now compatible with the current HTTP flow?
requestAndResponseContracts.answer: Yes for the core mutation contract. RequestData no longer freezes the entire envelope, so body parsing can populate requestData.body. ResponseData initializes cookie storage, and StageContext now safely mutates cookie fields even when they start empty. This removes several of the contract mismatches called out in the first report.

evidence.note: RequestData now keeps body writable while still preventing extension of the envelope.
ResponseData initializes cookie as an object instead of null.
StageContext safely reads and writes response body, status, headers, and cookies.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Body parsing can mutate requestData.body without violating the request envelope contract
Response cookie mutation is safe from the first write onward

pipelineCore.question: Is the request-pipeline core itself now compatible with gateway lifecycle and stage metadata tracking?
pipelineCore.answer: Yes. RequestPipeline now loads its adapter during construction instead of waiting for first request time, so the earlier frozen-instance adapter problem is gone. The core stage loop also tracks currentStageIndex and currentStageName against an executionContext.meta object that explicitly includes those fields.

evidence.note: The request pipeline loads its adapter during construction, avoiding the earlier frozen-object lazy-load mismatch.
The pipeline core provides ordered stage execution, break behavior, error fallback, and finish callback execution.
Execution metadata now includes currentStageIndex and currentStageName explicitly.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The pipeline adapter can be loaded safely with the current gateway lifecycle
Per-stage metadata tracking is compatible with the current execution context contract

bundledStageFlow.question: Do the bundled default HTTP stages currently form a working request progression?
bundledStageFlow.answer: Not yet. Several stage-level improvements are real: the static-file stage now streams assets through the response writer and the controller stage now maps RPC failures to 502. But the live stage sequence still contains one hard blocker: mid-queue-stage never returns true, so the pipeline stops after the first stage before static assets or controllers run. There is also a second mismatch in local-file-stream-stage, where cached-response coordination asks the manager for the literal question 'enqueue' instead of the configured queue question string 'queue'.

evidence.note: The active stage order is mid-queue-stage -> local-file-stream-stage -> tenant-controller-stage.
mid-queue-stage enqueues work but does not return true, so the pipeline core treats it as a break condition.
The static asset path itself is now implemented and can stream real files through the response writer.
Cached-response queue coordination still asks literal 'enqueue' and 'dequeue' instead of using the configured queue RPC names consistently.
Controller-stage RPC failure mapping to 502 Bad Gateway is in place.
The configured queue question names are queue/dequeue, not enqueue/dequeue.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The active first stage lets the pipeline continue when normal processing should proceed
The static asset stage is implemented as a real streaming stage
Controller RPC failures are translated into a deterministic HTTP fallback
Cached-response queue coordination uses the configured manager RPC question names

responseWritingAndAbortHandling.question: Can the response flow assemble and write status, headers, cookies, streams, and JSON safely?
responseWritingAndAbortHandling.answer: Mostly yes. The writer supports session-cookie generation, headers, streams, buffers, JSON objects, strings, and empty fallbacks, and the limiter now speaks both uWS and Node-style response objects safely. Abort handling and top-level 500 fallback are also still present. The remaining weakness is in body-read error mapping: the body catch block writes res.writeStatus(e) directly, which assumes the thrown value is already a valid uWS status string.

evidence.note: The response writer handles cookies, streams, buffers, JSON, strings, and write lifecycle hooks.
The limiter now supports uWS writeStatus/writeHeader and falls back to Node response APIs when needed.
Abort mapping, request end, and body-path error handling remain centralized in the main handler.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The active writer can emit stream, buffer, JSON, string, and empty responses
The rate limiter is compatible with the active uWS transport
Body-read errors are translated into a guaranteed valid HTTP status and payload

sessionAndStatefulFlow.question: Does session state now travel from manager to engine to tenant controllers correctly?
sessionAndStatefulFlow.answer: Only partially. The request flow does fetch session data before pipeline execution, and response-side session update plus cookie generation paths exist. But the fetched session payload is never assigned into executionContext.sessionData, and the tenant-controller RPC only forwards tenantRoute plus requestData. That means stateful controllers currently see cookies, but not the persisted session payload that was just fetched.

evidence.note: The handler requests session data before running the pipeline and then updates session data again after the pipeline.
Manager resolver returns session data, but does not itself attach it to executionContext.sessionData.
Tenant controller RPC currently sends tenantRoute and requestData only.
Tenant controller context contains requestData and tenantRoute, but not sessionData.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The engine fetches session data before controller work begins
The response path can persist updated session data and session cookies
Fetched session payload reaches tenant controller execution context

cachingAndManagerRoundtrips.question: Are route caching and response caching materially implemented today?
cachingAndManagerRoundtrips.answer: Only partially. Route resolution checks shared cache before asking the manager, and the file stage has shared-cache and queue coordination ideas for cached responses. But route lookup never writes back into cache after a miss, and the default pipeline does not currently include a stage that materializes and persists cached controller responses. So caching exists more as a scaffold than as a complete working path.

evidence.note: Route lookup reads from cache and falls back to manager RPC, but there is no write-back after a miss.
Response-cache lookup and queue coordination exist, but this stage does not persist a cached response artifact.
The default pipeline contains queue, static-file, and tenant-controller stages only; there is no cache-write stage in the current sequence.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Route resolution checks shared cache before asking manager
Route resolution writes route data back into shared cache after a miss
The default pipeline can reuse an already-persisted cached response artifact
The default pipeline materializes and persists new cached responses for later reuse

mustFix: Make mid-queue-stage return true on the normal path so the default pipeline does not stop before static assets and controllers run.
Pass fetched sessionData into executionContext and tenant controller context, not only cookies.
Align cached-response queue coordination with the configured manager RPC question names.
shouldFix: Write route data back into shared cache after a manager lookup miss.
Add a real cache-write stage if controller-response caching is intended for the first safe version.
Normalize body-read failures into a guaranteed valid HTTP status and payload instead of writing res.writeStatus(e) directly.
niceToHave: Add end-to-end HTTP smoke tests that cover redirect, static asset, wildcard controller, and session-enabled controller routes.
